title: Matrix-Product Belief Propagation for continuous-state-space variables
abstract: Computation of observables in discrete stochastic, possibly conditioned, dynamics over large sparse networks is at the basis of a myriad of applications. The Matrix-Product Belief Propagation method allows a semi-analytical estimation of such observables with a controlled error that depends on the size of the employed matrices, called bond size. Its computational cost is linear in the time horizon and the network size for a large family of models with discrete degrees of freedom. Here, a generalization of this method to models with continuous or mixed continuous/discrete degrees of freedom is presented, using a tunable expansion in a Hilbert function basis. The computational cost of the method is linear in the network size with a prefactor that depends on the basis size and the bond size. The method's efficacy is demonstrated by employing a Fourier basis for a mixed continuous/discrete representation of the Kinetic Ising dynamics with real-valued random couplings, where intermediate ``local fields'' are treated as continuous. The accuracy of the method is verified via comparison with Monte-Carlo simulations. For this model, we calculate time auto-correlations, time evolution of energy and magnetization, and finally we estimate the large deviation function of the magnetization at a given future time.
summary: The authors introduce Basis-MPBP, a numerical method that extends Matrix-Product Belief Propagation to systems with continuous state variables. By using a function basis expansion and MPS-based truncation, the method efficiently computes complex dynamical observables and large deviations in large-scale disordered networks. It serves as a computationally efficient alternative to Monte-Carlo simulations for studying rare events in continuous-state stochastic processes.
main_problem: Extending the Matrix-Product Belief Propagation (MPBP) method to handle stochastic dynamics involving continuous or mixed continuous/discrete state-space variables.
main_result: The development of Basis-MPBP, a method that achieves linear computational complexity in network size and time horizon while providing a controlled approximation for continuous variables via basis expansion.
method: A generalization of MPBP using a Matrix-Product State (MPS) ansatz combined with a tunable expansion in a Hilbert function basis (e.g., Fourier basis) and SVD-based truncation to manage bond dimension growth.
model_or_system: Kinetic Ising dynamics on large sparse networks (including tree, random-regular, and Erdős-Rényi graphs) with real-valued random couplings.
key_observables: Marginal distributions, time auto-correlations, time evolution of energy and magnetization, and large deviation functions of magnetization.
important_parameters: Bond size (matrix dimension), basis size (number of expansion elements), time horizon, and network degree.
main_assumptions: The method relies on the accuracy of the basis expansion and SVD truncation; it is exact on acyclic graphs only in the limit of infinite bond and basis size.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the redesign of a factor graph for high-degree nodes; Figures 5-7 display magnetization trajectories, autocorrelations, and the large deviation law on various graph topologies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of continuous-state dynamics, presents the Basis-MPBP formalism and its technical implementation via MPS and SVD, demonstrates its application to the Kinetic Ising model, and validates results against Monte-Carlo simulations and population dynamics.
why_interesting: The method provides a powerful tensor-network-inspired tool for simulating the non-equilibrium dynamics of continuous-variable systems, which is highly relevant for studying open quantum systems and many-body dynamics in the presence of disorder.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Representation of how a factor graph can be redesigned to deal with high-degree nodes. Panel a shows the original interaction network, panel b shows the dual representation of the factor graph (see [10], fig. 3). In panel c, the factor corresponding to node i is modified: the new construction uses only two types of simpler factors to perform all the operations that took place in the previous formulation.
FIG. 2: Two factors are needed: the triangular ones enforce the transformations from the original variables z to the new auxiliary variables y, the square ones provide the dynamical update.

FIG. 3: Random tree (N = 100) built with preferential attachment. β = 0.5, Jk,i ∼Uniform[−1, +1], hi = 0, m0 = 0.7. Top: magnetizations of all nodes at all times (Monte-Carlo vs MPBP with Fourier decomposition). Bottom: Time evolution of the “time-shifted energy” of the system.

FIG. 4: Random graph built with configuration model. β = 0.7, Jk,i ∼Uniform[−1, +1], hi = 0, m0 = 0.6. Top: magnetizations of all nodes at all times (Monte-Carlo vs MPBP with Fourier decomposition). Bottom: Time evolution of the “time-shifted energy” of the system.
FIG. 5: Average magnetization (left) and autocorrelation (right) on an infinite random-regular graph with degree 8 and with β = 0.3, Jk,i ∼Uniform[−1, +1], h = 0, m0 = 0.6.
